title: Claude Sonnet 4 and Opus 4 stop accepting API calls today
subtitle: Anthropic's first-generation 4 models hit end-of-life on the API at 9am Pacific; apps still calling the old identifiers will return errors with no silent fallback.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: At 9:00 AM Pacific today, Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 4 (claude-sonnet-4-20250514) and Claude Opus 4 (claude-opus-4-20250514) stop accepting API requests. There is no grace period and no automatic redirect to a successor — calls to those exact identifiers will return errors. Anthropic recommends pinning to Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Claude Opus 4.8 respectively, which is a one-line model-string change. Consumer Claude.ai and managed Claude Code environments are not affected, because Anthropic picks the model on the user's behalf there.

Today is also the day Anthropic's new programmatic-usage billing for the Claude Agent SDK and the claude-p binary kicks in. Eligible accounts have to claim a metered credit through their Claude account once, after which it refreshes each billing cycle. The Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.8 successor models cost more per token than the deprecated ones, so teams that didn't recompute their unit economics during the eight-week migration window will see the difference on the next invoice.

The pattern fits a broader 2026 trend: frontier labs are retiring models on a faster clock than enterprise procurement is used to. OpenAI sunset GPT-5.2 in ChatGPT last week and auto-migrated conversations to 5.5; Anthropic is doing the deletion version of the same move on the API side. The implicit contract — 'the model behind this name is the same model six months from now' — is being rewritten in real time, and the only protection developers have is pinning to exact dated identifiers and watching the deprecation calendar.

Takeaway for learners: read the model deprecation policy of any vendor you depend on, write the dates into your team calendar, and keep at least one prompt-level eval harness around that you can re-run against a new model the day you migrate. The next time a deprecation lands, you want to compare numbers, not vibes.
